fund.name: Whiteoak Capital Large & Mid Cap Fund Direct Plan Growth

allocations.rationale: Focuses on growing businesses in large and mid-sized companies — good balance of growth and stability. Fund manager has strong recent returns. WhiteOak is a smaller, focused fund house.

fund.name: Motilal Oswal Nifty Midcap 150 Index Fund - Direct Plan
fund.fund_house: Motilal Oswal Asset Management Company Limited

allocations.rationale: Low-cost index fund that tracks 150 mid-sized Indian companies. Midcaps have higher growth potential. Very low yearly fee means more of your money stays invested.

fund.name: BANDHAN SMALL CAP FUND - DIRECT PLAN GROWTH

allocations.rationale: Strong long-term returns in small companies. Small caps are riskier but offer highest growth over decades. Bandhan has a consistent team and solid history.

fund.name: Motilal Oswal S&P 500 Index Fund - Direct Plan Growth
fund.fund_house: Motilal Oswal Asset Management Company Limited

allocations.rationale: Gives you exposure to 500 top US companies like Apple, Microsoft, Amazon. Diversifies your portfolio globally. Very low yearly fee.

fund.name: ICICI Prudential Gold ETF
fund.fund_house: ICICI Prudential Asset Management Company Limited

allocations.rationale: Gold acts as a safety net during market crashes. ETF form is cheaper than physical gold. ICICI’s version is large and easy to buy/sell.

plan.setup_phase:
Step 1: Open a direct mutual fund account on Groww or Kuvera (free and takes 10 minutes with PAN and bank link).
Step 2: Start monthly SIPs in these five funds using the amounts shown (total ₹50,000).
Step 3: Park your ₹2 lakh emergency fund in a savings account like Yubi Max or Jupiter until built. Add ₹10,000/month there until you hit ₹6 lakh (target = 6 months of expenses).
review_checkpoints: After 6 months: confirm all SIPs are running. Check if emergency fund is on track.
After 1 year: compare each fund’s performance with similar funds. No need to change unless a fund is consistently underperforming its category.
Once a year: review your job income and increase SIP by 10% to grow wealth faster.
plan.rebalancing_guidelines: Check your portfolio every 12 months. If any category (e.g., small cap) grows to more than 10 percentage points above its target (e.g., reaches 30% when target is 20%), sell some and shift to underweight categories. This keeps risk in check.
plan.rationale: At 25 with a 30-year horizon, you can afford to take risks for higher rewards. Your ₹50,000/month SIP is fully in growth assets. We’ve used low-cost index funds for stability, added small and midcaps for aggressive growth, included US markets for global diversification, and gold for safety. Since you're in the new tax regime, we avoided ELSS and debt funds which offer no tax benefit here. This mix gives you a strong shot at building ₹10 crore by 55.
risks: Stock markets can drop 30-40% in a bad year — your portfolio value will fall temporarily. This is normal and recovers over time.
Small and midcap funds are more volatile — they can swing wildly up or down. Only long-term investors should stay put.
Global funds like S&P 500 are affected by dollar exchange rates and US interest rates beyond our control.
If you stop SIPs during downturns, you’ll miss the recovery. Stay consistent.
plan.disclaimer: For research/educational purposes only. Not certified financial advice.

sip_step_up.description: Increase SIP by 10% every year to match salary growth

allocation_schedule.trigger: After 10 years, slightly reduce equity and add safe debt
After 20 years, shift more to protection phase
Final 5 years: lower risk as goal nears

aps.reasoning: Equity sleeve is 75% of portfolio: 30% active Large-&-Mid-cap, 25% passive Nifty Midcap 150 index, 20% active Small-cap ⇒ 25/75 = 33% passive equity. No risk metrics supplied, but category labels show two true index funds (Midcap 150 & S&P 500) versus two active funds; therefore passive_instrument_fraction ≈ 0.27. Rebalancing only yearly with 10-percentage-point tolerance bands and rare review checkpoints → high turnover_score 0.85. Plan repeatedly cites ‘very low yearly fee’, uses direct plans and ETFs, compares expense ratios explicitly → cost_emphasis_score 0.75. Rationales mix cost reasoning with brief manager/team comments, leaning slightly toward cost but still mentioning fund-house quality → research_vs_cost_score 0.6. Strategy built for 30-year horizon, step-up SIPs, stay-the-course warnings → time_horizon_alignment_score 0.9. Portfolio activeness: absence of beta/tracking-error data forces reliance on categories; half the equity is active (high-expense mid/small-cap), half passive index, plus 10% gold ETF; blended activeness sits near middle, scored 0.45.

pqs.reasoning: Goal alignment: the 100 % growth-asset mix, 30-y horizon and 10 Cr target mesh perfectly with the investor’s aggressive risk appetite and long runway; glide-path shifts also mirror the “retire-by-55” objective. Diversification: broad across Indian L&M-cap, mid-cap index, small-cap, US large-cap and gold; only mild concentration in Motilal Oswal for both domestic mid-cap and US exposure and absence of dedicated debt until year 10 keep it from a perfect score. Risk-return appropriateness: projected 13 % CAGR base case is realistic for the high-equity blend; risks are candidly spelled out and match the aggressive stance. Internal consistency: allocations, rationale, step-up and glide path all tell the same equity-heavy story; tiny gap—no debt today yet glide path starts 10 % debt at year 10, but this is flagged as intentional future shift, so barely penalised. Tax efficiency: plan correctly skips ELSS (new regime, no 80C), keeps every fund equity-taxed (≥65 % equity) so gains face 12.5 % LTCG instead of slab rate; gold ETF is the only exception but is justified as tactical diversifier and still more tax-efficient than debt funds for this investor.
